skippedSources.name: Six Sigma Capital
skippedSources.reason: Trade ideas behind premium paywall; track record included in positioning item #29

items.title: "Run It Hot, Yet Again" — US fiscal spend above 2025 pace, global money printing strong, Fed structurally unable to hike
items.idea: Alf's core thesis: US nominal growth cycle is re-accelerating. Inflation-adjusted primary fiscal spending is running higher than at the same point in 2024-2025. Global real-economy money printing (G20 fiscal deficits + debt-funded AI capex) remains strong. The NY Fed probability of finding a job has stabilized, and weekly ADP job creation has swung from -50k (June 2025) to +42k today — pointing to a labor market bottom. The standard Fed reaction function would call for hikes, but with Warsh as Chair and needing 7 votes, a hike majority is structurally improbable.
items.angle: This is the macro scaffolding for everything: if Hormuz reopens and energy normalizes, the US is set to run hot with a Fed that is institutionally reluctant to tighten. Trade the spread between what the market prices and what Warsh will actually do.
items.tweetA: ADP just swung from -50k to +42k. Fiscal spend above last year. Global money printing strong. The data says hike. The FOMC says nope — Warsh needs 7 votes and won't get them. That gap is the entire macro trade.
items.tweetB: Markets are pricing a Fed hike for December. Meanwhile the new Chair is talking about using balance sheet shrinkage as cover to CUT and shifting the inflation benchmark to trimmed mean PCE (which is lower than core). "BoE-ization of the FOMC" is not a bear case. It's a positioning opportunity.

charts.desc: Charts: ADP series vs NY Fed job probability, fiscal spending comp, global M2 — all showing pick-up. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Gold 25-delta calls trading at DISCOUNT to puts — first time in years; market positioned for gold DROP not rally
items.idea: For the first time in many years, 25-delta gold call options are trading at a discount to 25-delta gold puts. Investors are paying more for protection against a gold decline than for participation in an upside move. Alf attributes this to AI dominance narrative crowding out commodity attention, combined with 'it's going nowhere' fatigue in precious metals. In the 'Run It Hot' regime his 40-year backtest identifies, gold is one of the best-performing assets — alongside silver, copper, small cap/EM equities.
items.angle: Gold skew inverted = structural consensus short-vol on gold upside. If the macro thesis plays (rates stay low, fiscal runs hot, nominal growth accelerates), the unwind of this positioning is violent. $GLD $SLV options are mis-priced.
items.tweetA: Gold 25-delta calls are trading BELOW puts for the first time in years. The market is more scared of gold going DOWN than up. In a world where the Fed won't hike, fiscal runs hot, and Hormuz reopens — that's the wrong bet. Gold skew inversion rarely persists.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching tech. Meanwhile, gold call vol just inverted — the market is paying up to hedge DOWNSIDE in precious metals. Last 40-year backtest says gold is the best trade in a 'run it hot' regime. Consensus is set up to be wrong.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Gold 25d risk reversal inverted — calls cheaper than puts. Clear visual signal of consensus positioning against gold upside.

items.title: "Run It Hot" trade playbook: high-beta equities, silver/copper, ZARJPY, curve steepeners
items.idea: From a 40-year backtest, the trades that outperform most in a 'run it hot' nominal growth regime: (1) high-beta equities — small cap, EM, value tilt; (2) high-beta commodities — silver, copper, and gold; (3) long cyclical FX / short defensive FX (e.g., long ZAR, short JPY); (4) curve steepeners and short long-duration bonds. The biggest pushback from large macro pod shops Alf met in London: skepticism on gold and small cap/EM/value. That skepticism itself is the signal.
items.angle: The 3 macro pods Alf met in London are the consensus short. When the smart money is wrong-footed in size on small cap and gold simultaneously, the mean-reversion can be brutal.
items.tweetA: Three large macro pod shops told Alf they have zero interest in gold or small cap. That's the trade. When the concentrated skepticism is on the highest-beta assets in a run-hot regime, the squeeze happens fast. $IWM $SLV $GDX $EEM.
items.tweetB: The 'run it hot' playbook is simple: long small cap/EM, long silver/copper, long ZARJPY, short 30Y. Every single one of these is under-owned right now relative to history. The AI megacap obsession has cleared the field.

items.title: 800VDC is inevitable: 1MW rack at 48V needs 200kg copper; 800V drops current 16.7x and I²R losses 278x
items.idea: The physics of 800VDC are not optional. At 48-54VDC, a 1MW rack requires ~200kg of copper busbars. At 600kW, a 48V approach would need ~64U-equivalent of power hardware. Moving to 800V cuts current by 16.7x (from ~12,500A to ~750A at 600kW), and I²R losses fall ~278x. In practice, operators capture ~5% facility-level efficiency gain — translating to 50MW of savings at 1GW IT load, worth tens of millions annually or equivalent to new compute capacity.
items.angle: The efficiency math is so compelling that 800VDC is not a feature — it's a cost imperative. Any supplier of traditional LV power equipment faces structural displacement. Any company making new components (SSTs, power racks, SiC power semiconductors) is seeing a multi-billion TAM open.
items.tweetA: A 1MW datacenter rack at 48V needs 200kg of copper. At 1GW scale that's hundreds of tons. 800VDC cuts current 16.7x. I²R losses fall 278x. This isn't an upgrade — it's a forced physics migration. $NVDA $AMAT $WOLF
items.tweetB: 800VDC saves 5% at the facility level. At 1GW of IT load = 50MW continuous savings. That's either $80-100M/year in electricity costs OR new compute capacity unlocked. The hyperscalers aren't doing this to be green. They're doing it because the math is brutal.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: I²R loss reduction at different voltages + copper mass comparison. Visual proof of 800VDC physics imperative. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: 800VDC 4-phase roadmap: sidecar TAM peaks $11B in 2028; power rack ASP $400-500k (10x legacy); 39GW by 2030
items.idea: SemiAnalysis maps 4 phases: Phase 1 (2026/2027) rack-level sidecar retrofit; Phase 2 (2027/2028) 800VDC-native compute; Phase 3 (late 2028/2029) facility-wide DC distribution; Phase 4 (>2029) SST end-state. Total 800VDC capacity expected ~39GW by 2030. HVDC power rack ASP: $400-500k (~10x legacy $40k). Sidecar TAM peaks ~$11B in 2028 before facility-level 800VDC takes share.
items.angle: The $11B peak sidecar TAM in 2028 is a concentrated 2-year window. Winners are companies already certified to Diablo 400 or Nvidia's spec. Delta, Advanced Energy, TE Connectivity appear in the spec as key components.
items.tweetA: The 800VDC power rack market peaks at $11B in 2028. A single unit costs $400-500k — 10x what it replaces. The entire hyperscaler capex cycle spawns a second-order equipment cycle that most people haven't modeled. $AEIS $VIAV
items.tweetB: 39GW of datacenter capacity on 800VDC by 2030. The migration window is 2026-2028 for the sidecar phase, then inflects to facility-wide DC in 2029. If you're building a position in AI infrastructure suppliers, phase-timing matters more than people think.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: 800VDC adoption curve, TAM by phase, MW penetration. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Nvidia sits OUTSIDE Diablo 400 spec — proprietary 660kW monopolar 800V design; Google/Meta/Microsoft already diverging within spec
items.idea: Despite co-authoring Diablo 400, Google runs 900kW with 12 AC whips, Meta at 800kW with 50kW cables, Amazon at 800kW on ±400V, Microsoft progressing slower. Nvidia is entirely outside this framework — developing monopolar 800V at 660kW, air-cooled samples mid-2026, liquid-cooled VR Ultra sampling late-2026.
items.angle: Fragmentation is a feature, not a bug, for component suppliers selling into multiple spec ecosystems. The multi-vendor interoperability Diablo 400 promised is breaking down in practice within 12 months of publication.
items.tweetA: Diablo 400 was supposed to standardize 800VDC. Instead, Google, Meta, and Microsoft are each running divergent designs within 12 months. And Nvidia isn't even in the spec — building its own 660kW monopolar reference. Datacenter electrical infrastructure is fragmenting, not consolidating.
items.tweetB: Nvidia's Kyber rack at 660kW runs its own 800VDC spec. Google is at 900kW. Meta at 800kW. Amazon at 800kW. Four different designs, four different supplier requirements. The hyperscalers agreed on a standard and immediately customized away from it. This is not a commoditization story.

charts.desc: Charts: SemiAnalysis power rack architecture comparison across hyperscalers. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Solid-State Transformers: 40x weight reduction, 14x size reduction vs iron-core; Wolfspeed 10kV SiC now commercially available (March 2026)
items.idea: Phase 4 (>2029): SSTs replace conventional transformers. SSTs convert directly from medium voltage (13.8-45kV) to 800VDC using SiC MOSFETs. Up to 15% total system efficiency improvement. Core shrinks ~90% (50/60Hz → 20,000Hz+). Infineon claims 40x weight, 14x size reduction. Wolfspeed 10kV SiC MOSFET commercially available as bare die since March 2026, enabling the Phase 3-to-4 bridge.
items.angle: SST TAM is enormous — every datacenter transformer in the world becomes a replacement candidate over the next decade. Infineon, DG Matrix, and SiC power device makers are positioned early in a multi-decade upgrade cycle.
items.tweetA: Wolfspeed just released a 10kV SiC MOSFET commercially (March 2026). That's the part enabling direct medium-voltage-to-800VDC conversion in datacenters. Infineon claims the resulting SST is 40x lighter, 14x smaller than a conventional transformer. Every datacenter transformer is a replacement candidate. $WOLF $IFNNY
items.tweetB: Solid-State Transformers: same job as a 2-ton iron-core transformer, but fraction of the space. 15% efficiency gain. Programmable. Bidirectional. The 800VDC migration doesn't end at the rack — it ends at the grid connection. This is a decade-long replacement cycle for $AMETEK $ABB $HUBB.

charts.desc: Charts: SST architecture, Infineon size comparison, Wolfspeed SiC roadmap. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: LV UPS systems to become obsolete in 800VDC era — battery rack replaces power rack at ~$200k/MW; Vertiv/Eaton at structural risk
items.idea: In Phase 3, the $500k/MW power rack is replaced by a simpler $200k/MW battery rack (BBU + supercapacitors only). Traditional centralized LV UPS systems lose their role — Google and Meta already bypassed them with distributed BBUs. Colo providers keep LV UPS for mixed-workload flexibility. ABB's MV UPS HiPerGuard runs at 98% efficiency, deployed at Applied Digital's 400MW North Dakota campus. BESS increasingly replaces diesel generators.
items.angle: Vertiv and Eaton have significant LV UPS revenue at risk. ABB's Nvidia partnership on MV distribution is prescient positioning. The colo provider moat for traditional UPS is shrinking.
items.tweetA: Traditional UPS systems are being bypassed in next-gen AI datacenters. Google and Meta skipped them years ago. Phase 3 of 800VDC makes them structurally redundant for hyperscalers. If you own $VRT or $ETN on UPS revenue — that business is shrinking, not growing.
items.tweetB: The $200k/MW battery rack replaces the $500k/MW power rack in 800VDC Phase 3 — but the UPS disappears entirely. ABB's MV UPS at 98% efficiency (deployed at Applied Digital's 400MW North Dakota campus) is what's winning. The datacenter power equipment pecking order is being rewritten.

charts.desc: Charts: distributed vs centralized UPS comparison, battery rack architecture. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Iran-US deal = Republican senators call it 'surrender' — no regime change, no nuclear shift, Iran may still charge for Strait access
items.idea: The emerging US-Iran deal to reopen Hormuz involves no change of regime, no nuclear shift, and without measures preventing Iran from charging for Strait access in future. Republican senators characterize this as 'something very close to surrender.' Authers: the destruction of the last three months 'will have won the US nothing.' Long-term geopolitical implications troubling for Pax Americana and deglobalization premium.
items.angle: If Iran retains right to charge for Strait access, this sets a precedent for every chokepoint globally. The market is pricing a clean re-opening — it's not pricing the new toll-road model.
items.tweetA: The US-Iran deal apparently lets Iran KEEP charging for Strait of Hormuz access. No regime change, no nuclear shift. Republican senators call it surrender. The market is rallying on 'peace.' But if Iran can charge tolls on the world's most critical shipping lane going forward — what exactly did we buy?
items.tweetB: Brent is down 6% on Iran deal optimism. But read the fine print: Iran retains toll authority over Hormuz going forward. The oil risk premium may collapse, but the deglobalization premium just went up. Long crude put spreads, short geopolitical complacency.

charts.desc: Chart: Brent December/June 2027 forward curve drop on deal news. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Pope Leo XIV encyclical on AI: factory workers recording movements via smart glasses to train robots that replace them
items.idea: Factory workers wear smart glasses recording hand movements — the data becomes training for robots that replace them. Worker Ashish Narayan: 'it feels like working in your own grave, while you make your own casket.' Pope Leo XIV issued an encyclical warning against delegating decisions affecting 'employment, credit, access to public services or reputation' to automated systems lacking compassion.
items.angle: The smart-glasses factory training loop is the clearest illustration of labor displacement dynamics. It's also a regulatory risk for companies deploying at scale — Leo's framing will shape EU and developing-market policy.
items.tweetA: Factory workers are being paid to wear smart glasses that record their hand movements. That data trains the robots that will replace them. 'It feels like working in your own grave, while you make your own casket.' The Pope noticed. Regulators will follow.
items.tweetB: 'Working in your own grave while you make your own casket.' That is how a factory worker in India described his job — recording his movements via smart glasses to train his own replacement. Pope Leo XIV just made this mainstream. Europe will regulate it within 18 months.

charts.desc: Chart: Global X Robotics ETF vs market — current rally impressive but below pandemic peak relative performance.

items.title: Physical AI bifurcation: US leads brains (LLMs, chips); China dominates body (actuators = 50% of humanoid cost, rare earth-dependent)
items.idea: Alpine Macro and Barclays both identify bifurcated physical AI landscape. US leads LLMs and semiconductor 'brains.' China dominates 'body' — actuators account for ~50% of humanoid production costs vs 35% brains and 15% batteries. Actuators are highly dependent on magnetic rare earths where China holds near-monopoly. Chinese IT stocks have outperformed even US tech under Trump 2.0.
items.angle: The US advantage in AI compute does not translate to physical AI dominance. China's rare earth + manufacturing moat in actuators is structural. The robotics trade is not $NVDA — it's Chinese actuator makers and rare earth suppliers.
items.tweetA: Humanoid robots: 50% of production cost is actuators. Actuators need magnetic rare earths. China controls rare earths AND dominates manufacturing. The US 'wins AI' narrative falls apart the moment you leave the cloud. Physical AI is a Chinese supply chain story.
items.tweetB: Everyone is pricing the 'US wins AI' narrative. But physical AI requires actuators (50% of humanoid cost) that depend on rare earths China controls. The US advantage in LLMs doesn't translate to robotics. That's why Chinese IT has outperformed US tech under Trump 2.0.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Chinese IT stocks vs US tech under Trump 2.0. The divergence is clear and surprising. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Morgan Stanley: AI diffusion 2x faster than internet era WITHOUT triggering unemployment spike yet — greatest risk is diffusion moving too fast
items.idea: Morgan Stanley survey finds baseline AI diffusion is 2x the speed of the internet era, without triggering large unemployment rise or downturn. However, greatest risk: allowing diffusion to move too fast — rapid adoption can create skill mismatch overwhelming labor market flexibility. The absence of visible unemployment impact may be creating a false sense of security.
items.angle: The 'no unemployment spike yet' data point is being read as green light. Morgan Stanley's risk flag — skill mismatch at speed — is the bear case that hasn't appeared in data yet. This is the 1999 equivalent of 'the productivity miracle is real.'
items.tweetA: AI is diffusing 2x faster than the internet era. Unemployment hasn't spiked — yet. Morgan Stanley's risk flag: when diffusion outpaces skill adaptation, the mismatch is sudden and violent. We may be building that pressure right now without the release valve being visible in the data.
items.tweetB: 'No unemployment spike yet' is doing a lot of work in the AI narrative. At 2x internet diffusion speed, we have half the time to build adaptive institutions. The internet took 20 years to rewire the labor market. AI doesn't get 20 years.

charts.desc: Chart: AI diffusion speed vs internet diffusion speed comparison. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: China could offset 60% of its labor force decline by 2035 with robots (Barclays) — demographic imperative for robotics adoption
items.idea: Barclays: 8 million factories, 2.8 billion buildings globally — cheaper to adapt robots to human environments than to rebuild the world. China's specific demographic driver: robotics could offset up to 60% of its labor force decline by 2035, creating a policy-level imperative for adoption that does not exist in the same form in the US or Europe.
items.angle: China's 60% labor force offset target is a government-level adoption mandate disguised as a demographic fact. When government survival depends on robotics adoption, the investment curve is not optional and not gradual.
items.tweetA: China needs robots to offset 60% of its labor force decline by 2035. This isn't a venture bet. It's demographic arithmetic. When government survival depends on robotics adoption, the investment curve is not optional and not gradual. Factor that into your China tech thesis.
items.tweetB: 8 million factories, 2.8 billion buildings. The reason humanoids look like humans is straightforward: it's cheaper to build robots that fit existing environments than to rebuild the world for robots. China has 10 years to replace 60% of its workforce. That timeline makes US robotics adoption look leisurely.

charts.desc: Chart: China labor force decline projection vs robotics offset estimate. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Bitcoin sentiment at 'Peak Apathy' — social media metrics at absolutely dire levels; but extreme frustration has never been seen at bull market tops
items.idea: CheckOnChain published 'Peak Apathy' analysis — Bitcoin holders at their most frustrated, annoyed, and disappointed in the current cycle. Key insight: extreme frustration and boredom in sentiment is historically NOT what you see at bull market tops — tops are characterized by euphoria, not apathy. Michael Sullivan distinguishes retail sentiment cohorts from large Bitcoin personas (Saylor etc.). The contrarian read: sentiment trough consistent with accumulation phases.
items.angle: When quality on-chain analysts track sentiment as primary signal and call it 'Peak Apathy,' that's the setup that precedes the move nobody is positioned for. The social media data is the contrarian buy signal.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin sentiment is at 'Peak Apathy' right now. CheckOnChain has the social media data — Bitcoiners are frustrated, annoyed, disappointed. The note is worth reading for one sentence: 'bad and boring sentiment is not what you tend to see at bull market tops.'
items.tweetB: I keep watching the Bitcoin sentiment data. The social media signal is at a frustration extreme that historically marks accumulation phases, not distribution phases. The price is flat. The HODLers are angry. That combination has a name, and it's not 'top.'

charts.desc: Podcast episode — charts inside article, worth opening.

items.title: Last EIA report = LARGEST INVENTORY DECLINE ON RECORD — Strait confirmed closed through June; Iran holding Hormuz + uranium as negotiation levers
items.idea: MacroEdge dismisses weekend 'deal' headlines as unverified anonymous Arabic media sources. Actual analysis: US domestic inventory drawdown -1mmbpd. Last week's EIA = largest inventory decline on record. Strait confirmed closed through at least June. Iran unlikely to surrender both Hormuz and uranium simultaneously. Chinese increasingly involved. Administration may actually benefit from delay — July creates physical shortage inevitability.
items.angle: The jawboning is working to suppress prices. The physical reality is decoupling. When the physical shortage hits (targeting July), the gap between narrative and fundamentals closes violently.
items.tweetA: Last week's EIA inventory draw was the largest on record. The Strait is confirmed closed through June. And the 'deal' headlines this weekend were anonymous texts through Arabic media — no verification, no parade. The physical oil market is not waiting for diplomacy.
items.tweetB: Iran's negotiating position: keep Hormuz closed AND uranium enrichment. The Chinese are in the room but not solving it. By July, inventory depletion forces the issue. The administration has been running the 'deal soon' narrative for weeks. The EIA data doesn't care about narratives.

charts.desc: WASDE wheat data and WTI curve. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: US winter wheat: smallest crop since 1965 — production collapsed 25% to 1.048B bushels; Texas Panhandle dryland 'fundamentally broken'
items.idea: USDA May 12 WASDE: US smallest winter wheat crop since 1965, total production projected to collapse 25% to 1.048 billion bushels. On-the-ground from Texas Panhandle: 'fundamentally broken winter wheat crop on the dryland side.' Irrigated acres acting as 'life support.' Wheat futures down slightly from cycle highs — but not nearly as much as crude oil — reflecting ongoing tightness independent of Hormuz.
items.angle: Wheat + Hormuz closed = food supply pressure from two directions simultaneously. Wheat hasn't corrected with oil — the market already knows wheat fundamentals are independent of Hormuz.
items.tweetA: US winter wheat is on track for the smallest crop since 1965 — 1.048B bushels, down 25%. On-the-ground from Texas Panhandle: 'fundamentally broken' dryland crop. Wheat futures haven't followed oil down today. Food inflation is structural, not Hormuz-driven. $WEAT $ZW_F
items.tweetB: USDA confirmed it: Texas Panhandle wheat — fundamentally broken. Smallest crop since 1965. Down 25%. This is the agricultural equivalent of the oil inventory story — quietly building toward a supply shock. $WEAT $ZW_F

charts.desc: WASDE comparison chart and wheat futures curve. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: US-Iran direct military exchange May 25: CENTCOM airstrikes inside Iran; Iran fired on US warships in Gulf of Oman — second exchange in 18 days
items.idea: May 25: CENTCOM confirmed airstrikes on Iranian missile launch sites and fast-attack boats in southern Iran — 'self-defense' during 'ongoing ceasefire.' Iranian sources: two IRGC fast boats struck, Iran fired on US warships in Gulf of Oman, air defenses activated east of Bandar Abbas. Explosions reported in Bandar Abbas, Sirik, Jask. This follows May 7-8 exchange. Both sides framing as defensive and limited.
items.angle: Active military exchanges happening WHILE both sides call it a 'ceasefire.' Second exchange in 18 days signals the ceasefire is nominal at best. This is the market risk underpriced in the peace-dividend trade.
items.tweetA: CENTCOM confirmed airstrikes INSIDE Iran on May 25 — during an 'ongoing ceasefire.' Iran fired back on US warships in the Gulf of Oman. Air defenses activated near Bandar Abbas. This is the second US-Iran exchange in 18 days. The market is buying a 'deal.' The military is still at it.
items.tweetB: The peace-dividend trade is pricing a deal. The military reality: US and Iran exchanged fire 18 days apart. Both calling it 'defensive.' Both calling it 'limited.' The gap between market narrative and military reality is the position.

items.title: Samsung 45K-worker strike begins May 26 for 18 days — biggest semiconductor work stoppage in history; HBM and DRAM supply at risk
items.idea: 45,000-worker Samsung strike begins today (May 26) lasting 18 days. Characterized as the biggest semiconductor work stoppage in history, creating a chokepoint for HBM and DRAM production. Samsung is the dominant global supplier of HBM3 and HBM3E for AI training clusters. Timing: arriving into $725B hyperscaler capex cycle.
items.angle: Samsung's HBM is irreplaceable short-term for AI training. An 18-day strike at the world's largest memory manufacturer, during the biggest capex cycle in history, is exactly the supply shock that hasn't been priced. $MU $SK Hynix beneficiaries.
items.tweetA: 45,000 Samsung workers just went on strike. 18 days. It's the biggest semiconductor work stoppage in history — hitting HBM and DRAM production at exactly the moment hyperscalers are spending $725B on AI capex. Supply shock into demand supercycle. $MU should gap up. $SSNLF faces a different week.
items.tweetB: Samsung 45K-worker strike: 18 days. HBM is the bottleneck for AI training. Samsung makes most of it. SK Hynix benefits directly. Micron benefits. The market was focused on NVDA earnings last week. Today's trade is the memory supply disruption the strike creates.

items.title: SpaceX S-1 filed — $1.5T+ valuation; June 11-12 pricing; largest IPO in history with 18,712 BTC on balance sheet
items.idea: SpaceX S-1 absorbed over weekend. Valuation $1.5T+. Pricing June 11-12. At $1.5T, this would be the largest IPO in history by a substantial margin. S-1 includes 18,712 BTC ($1.29B fair value) on balance sheet — institutional adoption proof at unprecedented scale.
items.angle: $1.5T IPO in 3 weeks. The BTC holding on the SpaceX balance sheet is a bigger institutional adoption signal than any ETF inflow. The rotation risk when this prices is enormous.
items.tweetA: SpaceX is pricing in 3 weeks at $1.5T+. Largest IPO in history. Buried in the S-1: 18,712 BTC on the balance sheet ($1.29B). We're at the point where the largest IPO in history is also a Bitcoin treasury company. That's a sentence I didn't expect to write.
items.tweetB: $1.5T SpaceX IPO pricing June 11-12. Toyota is $240B. Walmart is $730B. SpaceX would debut larger than any non-tech company ever. The S-1 gets absorbed quietly over a holiday weekend and the market trades oil and Iran. The rotation risk when this prices is enormous.

items.title: NVDA Q1: $81.6B (+85% YoY), Q2 guide $91B, $80B buyback, 25x dividend hike — hyperscaler 2026 capex revised UP to $725B; sovereign AI tripled to $30B
items.idea: NVDA Q1: $81.6B revenue (+85% YoY), Q2 guide $91B, $80B buyback, 25x dividend hike. Hyperscaler 2026 combined capex revised up to $725B. Sovereign AI capex tripled to $30B. Cisco raised AI orders pipeline from $5B to $9B. NVDA-Anthropic partnership 'scaling very, very quickly' per Huang.
items.angle: $725B hyperscaler + $30B sovereign = $755B. US defense budget is ~$860B. AI infrastructure is now on the scale of national defense. The downstream equipment supply chain has not been re-rated for this.
items.tweetA: Hyperscaler 2026 capex: $725B. Sovereign AI: $30B. Combined = $755B. The US defense budget is $860B. We are building AI infrastructure at the scale of a military-industrial complex — and that's just the compute layer. The power, cooling, and networking equipment markets haven't been re-rated yet.
items.tweetB: NVDA Q1: $81.6B revenue. Up 85% YoY. Q2 guide: $91B. Buyback: $80B. Dividend: 25x hike. This isn't a beat — it's a demonstration that the demand is structural and growing. Cisco's AI pipeline went from $5B to $9B. Every infrastructure supplier is being pulled into this gravity well.

items.title: BTC broke $75K Saturday ($917M liquidations), 6-day ETF outflow -$1.25B — May 29 $6B options expiry; put/call 0.41; $82K call wall
items.idea: BTC broke $75K Saturday — first time in over a month. $917M+ in liquidations. ETF flows negative for 6 consecutive days totaling -$1.25B. Deribit put/call ratio dropped to 0.41 with call OI clustering at $79K. Break above $79K triggers delta-hedging acceleration toward $82K May 29 expiry call wall ($6B notional). Cathie Wood Sunday: 'shift FROM gold TO Bitcoin.' Bitmine (Tom Lee) bought 60K ETH ($126M) in 24 hours.
items.angle: The $79K gamma level is a mechanical trigger — break it and market makers are forced to buy. Setup: negative flows + sentiment low + gamma trap = asymmetric upside if $79K breaks before May 29.
items.tweetA: BTC put/call ratio: 0.41. Call OI stacked at $79K. May 29 $6B expiry with $82K call wall. The market just spent a week liquidating (-$1.25B ETF outflows, $917M liquidations Saturday). Now there's a gamma trap above. $79K is the trigger.
items.tweetB: Cathie Wood: 'shift from gold to Bitcoin.' Bitmine (Tom Lee) bought $126M of ETH in 24 hours. BTC put/call at 0.41 — heavily skewed calls. Sentiment at 'Peak Apathy.' Three independent signals pointing the same direction. The May 29 expiry is in 3 days.

items.title: Brent -6% to $96.31, WTI <$91 on deal optimism — still +49% YoY; Goldman $90 late-2026; IEA 'RED ZONE' by July; Wood Mac $80 if Hormuz reopens
items.idea: Brent fell ~6% to $96.31; WTI below $91 — biggest single-day drop of the war. Context: Brent still +49.42% YoY. Goldman Sachs raised late-2026 Brent forecast to $90 (from $80). IEA: 'RED ZONE' by July if disruption persists. Wood Mackenzie: $80 YE 2026 IF Hormuz fully reopens. Hassett (NEC Director): 'negative inflation' possible from energy crash — framing deal as administration's primary inflation tool.
items.angle: Wood Mac $80 = -17% additional downside from current $96. Goldman $90 = still below current spot. If deal fails, back to $110+. The risk/reward for buying energy dips is better than consensus thinks.
items.tweetA: Brent -6% to $96 on Iran deal optimism. Wood Mackenzie's target IF Hormuz fully reopens: $80. Goldman: $90 late-2026. The market is rallying on a deal that hasn't happened, negotiated between parties who were exchanging fire 24 hours ago. IEA says 'RED ZONE' in 6 weeks if nothing resolves.
items.tweetB: Brent is still +49% year-over-year after today's 6% drop. The NEC Director says 'negative inflation' is possible if energy crashes. The IEA says 'RED ZONE' in 6 weeks if it doesn't. Every policymaker wants the same outcome. That consensus creates fragile positioning on the downside.

charts.desc: Chart: Brent December + June 2027 forward curve drop. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Intuit cuts 17% workforce — AI displacement confirmed at scale; Cisco AI orders pipeline raised from $5B to $9B
items.idea: Intuit cutting 17% of its workforce is AI displacement at a knowledge-work company — not a factory floor. Simultaneously, Cisco raised AI orders pipeline from $5B to $9B, confirming the capex cycle is real and broadening into enterprise networking. The productivity-displacement dynamic in clear expression: companies cutting white-collar workers while spending more on AI infrastructure.
items.angle: Intuit 17% cut = first major SaaS company doing the AI-displacement pivot publicly. Others will follow quietly. Cisco $9B AI pipeline confirms the NVDA capex cycle is transmitting downstream. $CSCO $JNPR are secondary beneficiaries.
items.tweetA: Intuit just cut 17% of its workforce. This is not a factory. It's a software company replacing knowledge workers with AI. Meanwhile Cisco's AI orders pipeline went from $5B to $9B. Two sides of the same coin: AI reduces headcount, AI increases infrastructure spend. The net for GDP is the question nobody has answered.
items.tweetB: Cisco's AI orders: $5B → $9B. That's the NVDA capex cycle transmitting downstream into networking. Every router and switch in a new datacenter is a Cisco (or Arista or Juniper) sale. Intuit cutting 17% proves the AI is real. Cisco's order book proves the build-out is real.

items.title: Tokenized gold crosses $5B AUM — first major on-chain commodity milestone; ETH holds $2,100 for 10 sessions; Bitmine adds 60K ETH ($126M)
items.idea: Tokenized gold AUM crossed $5B — first major on-chain commodity to hit this threshold. ETH held $2,100 for 10 consecutive sessions. Bitmine (Tom Lee) added 60K ETH ($126M) in 24 hours, total 5.33M ETH. SEC delayed tokenized stock exemption decision. Trump May 20 EO: 120-day Fed review of crypto firms' Reserve Bank access.
items.angle: Tokenized gold at $5B + ETH accumulation ($126M in 24h) happening simultaneously with Cathie Wood 'gold to BTC' pivot = narrative tension about where institutional commodity hedging lives in the new architecture.
items.tweetA: Tokenized gold just crossed $5B AUM on-chain. Tom Lee's Bitmine bought $126M of ETH in 24 hours. Cathie Wood says 'shift from gold to BTC.' Three separate actors, three separate theses, all moving the same direction: hard assets are migrating to digital rails. The $5B milestone is the proof of concept.
items.tweetB: ETH has defended $2,100 for 10 consecutive sessions. Bitmine added 60K ETH. Tokenized gold hits $5B. The narrative is shifting from 'is crypto real' to 'which protocol wins the institutional commodity layer.' ETH's DeFi moat matters for the tokenization thesis.

items.title: US Commerce Dept investing $2B+ in quantum infrastructure — taking minority equity stakes in 9 companies including IBM and GlobalFoundries
items.idea: US Commerce Department investing $2B+ in quantum computing infrastructure, taking minority non-controlling equity stakes in 9 quantum companies — IBM and GlobalFoundries among named recipients. Government equity stakes, not just grants. Similar to CHIPS Act mechanics but extended into quantum.
items.angle: Government equity stakes create a new category of backstopped investment. IBM's quantum division and GlobalFoundries get federal balance sheet support. The equity model vs grant model signals longer-term engagement.
items.tweetA: US Commerce Dept is taking equity stakes — not just grants — in 9 quantum companies. IBM and GlobalFoundries are in the portfolio. When the government takes equity, it's not just funding: it's backstopping a sector. This is the CHIPS Act model applied to quantum. $IBM $GFS
items.tweetB: $2B+ in quantum infrastructure and the government is taking equity in 9 companies. The word is 'equity.' Grants are one-time. Equity means the government needs these companies to succeed, which changes how regulatory and procurement decisions get made.

items.title: Warsh Day 4 as Fed Chair — first FOMC June 16-17; April PCE Thursday: BofA forecasts +0.4% headline, +0.3% core MoM
items.idea: Kevin Warsh is 4 days into tenure as Fed Chair. First FOMC June 16-17. BofA forecasts April PCE at +0.4% headline MoM, +0.3% core MoM — 'single biggest tape risk this week.' If PCE prints +0.5%, the entire peace-dividend trade resets violently. Bond market already 'staunchly rejected the war last week' per MacroEdge — Warsh's navigation extremely constrained.
items.angle: Warsh walks into his first FOMC with a hot PCE print on Thursday, a peace dividend trade distorting energy prices, and a market that has priced him as a closet dove. The first meeting is a credibility test on two sides simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh's first FOMC is June 16-17. His first major data point is April PCE this Thursday — BofA expects +0.4% headline, +0.3% core. The peace-dividend trade is pricing in lower energy costs. PCE doesn't care about deals. If it prints +0.5%, the reset is violent. Welcome to the chair, Kevin.
items.tweetB: April PCE Thursday. BofA: +0.4% headline MoM. The market is celebrating Iran optimism and lower oil. PCE data was locked before the weekend. The gap between what the market is pricing and what's in the data creates the week's asymmetric risk. Position light into Thursday.

items.title: 200 years of bear markets: current setup = sovereign balance sheet risk (NOT commercial like 2008) + 1970s inflation overlay; three archetypes all present
items.idea: Capital Flows Macro analyzed 200 years of bear market history across three archetypes: cyclical, event-driven, and structural. Today's setup has elements of all three but doesn't cleanly fit any. Critical distinction from 2008: that was commercial balance sheet risk (private leverage blowup). Today is sovereign balance sheet risk and currency repricing. All large equity bear markets compress into specific factor unwinds — knowing the actual factor lets you hedge directly, not reduce entire book.
items.angle: The '2008 analog' most people are working from is wrong — this is sovereign balance sheet risk. The correct analog is probably early-1970s stagflation with a modern financial system overlay. The factor unwind, if it comes, will be in duration/rates, not credit spreads.
items.tweetA: 2008 was commercial leverage blowing up. Today's setup is sovereign leverage and currency repricing — fundamentally different mechanism. The bear market analogy determines your hedge. If you're hedging like it's 2008 (credit spreads, bank stress), you're hedging the wrong tail.
items.tweetB: Three bear market archetypes: cyclical, event-driven, structural. Current setup has elements of all three PLUS the 1970s inflation overlay. Most people misdiagnose the regime and take positions that hurt them. The question is not 'which analog' — it's 'what are the actual drivers.'

charts.desc: Slide deck from livestream — bear market archetype comparison. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: PURR: only crypto treasury with net positive on underlying — Hyperliquid Strategies up $1B on HYPE; buy-back at NAV discount / issue at premium mechanics
items.idea: PURR (Hyperliquid treasury company) is already up $1B on its HYPE position — structurally unique vs MicroStrategy. PURR buys back stock when trading at NAV discount, issues at premium — capital allocation mechanics most investors aren't modeling. Hyperliquid provides 'ISDA-level leverage to non-institutional players,' changing the global cost of leverage.
items.angle: The buy-back-at-discount/issue-at-premium mechanic is a self-correcting NAV machine. Combined with being in-the-money on underlying, PURR is structurally differentiated from every other crypto treasury vehicle.
items.tweetA: PURR is the only crypto treasury company in the world where the treasury is ALREADY UP — $1B profit on HYPE. MicroStrategy is still in the red on its BTC cost basis. PURR also buys back at NAV discount and issues at premium. This is not the same trade as MSTR. The mechanics are fundamentally different.
items.tweetB: Hyperliquid provides 'ISDA-level leverage to non-institutional players.' That's the thesis in one sentence. If you believe leverage costs will converge across institutional and retail — and that crypto rails are the mechanism — then HYPE is a long-duration bet on the repricing of global financial access.

charts.desc: Slide deck: bear market playbook and PURR NAV mechanics. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Six Sigma recent track record: ASTS +50% (74→111), FLY +63% (32→52), NOK +29% (12.4→16) — technical entries on SMA 200, IPO AVWAP, EMA 20 retests
items.idea: Six Sigma published track record for recent weeks: ASTS from 74 to 111 (+50%, entered on SMA 200 reclaim at 72), FLY from 32 to 52 (+63%, entered on IPO AVWAP retest/hold), NOK 12.4→16 (+29%), CRWV 101→117 (+16%), CIFR 18.5→23 (+24%), CRCL 109→130 (+19%). Entry methodology: SMA 200 reclaim, IPO AVWAP retest, EMA 20 retest. Actual week-of-May-25 trade ideas are behind premium Discord paywall.
items.angle: The track record validates the technical framework. Winners cluster around AI infrastructure adjacencies: ASTS (satellite comms), NOK (telecom infra), CRWV (AI compute). The technicals are cleanest where analyst coverage is thinnest.
items.tweetA: ASTS: 74 to 111 on SMA 200 reclaim. NOK: 12.4 to 16. FLY: 32 to 52 on IPO AVWAP hold. The AI capex cycle is generating momentum setups in second-derivative plays — satellite comms, telecom infra, smaller AI names. The technicals tell you where the fundamental money is flowing.
items.tweetB: Six Sigma's recent winners cluster around AI infrastructure adjacencies: $ASTS (satellite), $NOK (telecom), $CRWV (compute). Not $NVDA, $MSFT, or $AMZN — the beneficiaries one step removed. That's where the technical setups are cleanest because analyst coverage is thinnest.
